benchmark: Demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations as outlined in safety regulations
item_specs: Students identify and apply lab safety procedures in science investigations
essential_questions: Why is lab safety important?
What safety procedures should you follow during investigations?
learning_targets: I can identify and follow lab safety rules
I can demonstrate safe practices during investigations
sample_assessment:
When heating a substance in a test tube, you should:
A) Point the opening toward yourself to observe
B) Point the opening away from yourself and others
C) Hold the test tube with your bare hands
D) Leave the test tube unattended

benchmark: Plan and implement comparative and descriptive investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, and using appropriate equipment
item_specs: Students design and conduct investigations using proper scientific methodology
essential_questions: How do you design a fair scientific investigation?
What makes a question testable?
learning_targets: I can plan a comparative investigation with a control and variables
I can ask testable questions and make observations
sample_assessment:
In an experiment testing how light affects plant growth, what is the independent variable?
A) The height of the plant
B) The amount of light
C) The type of soil
D) The amount of water

benchmark: Identify the components of the Earth's system, including the geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere, and describe their interactions
item_specs: Students identify and describe interactions between Earth's four spheres
essential_questions: How do Earth's systems interact?
What are the four major Earth systems?
learning_targets: I can identify the four Earth systems
I can describe how Earth's systems interact with each other
sample_assessment:
A volcanic eruption sends ash into the air and lava flows into the ocean. Which Earth systems are interacting?
A) Geosphere and biosphere only
B) Geosphere, atmosphere, and hydrosphere
C) Atmosphere and biosphere only
D) Hydrosphere only

benchmark: Describe the physical properties, including mass, magnetism, physical state, relative density, solubility, and the ability to conduct or insulate electrical and thermal energy
item_specs: Students identify and describe physical properties of matter
essential_questions: What physical properties can be used to identify substances?
How do physical properties help classify matter?
learning_targets: I can describe the physical properties of matter
I can use physical properties to classify and identify substances
sample_assessment:
Which property describes how well a substance dissolves in water?
A) Density
B) Magnetism
C) Solubility
D) Conductivity

benchmark: Classify resources from the Earth as renewable, nonrenewable, or inexhaustible and describe how human activity impacts Earth's resources
item_specs: Students classify resources and analyze human impact on resource availability
essential_questions: What is the difference between renewable and nonrenewable resources?
How do human activities impact Earth's resources?
learning_targets: I can classify resources as renewable, nonrenewable, or inexhaustible
I can describe how human activity impacts natural resources
sample_assessment:
Which resource is classified as nonrenewable?
A) Solar energy
B) Wind energy
C) Natural gas
D) Timber

benchmark: Describe how energy is transferred through transverse and longitudinal waves
item_specs: Students compare transverse and longitudinal waves and describe energy transfer
essential_questions: How do waves transfer energy?
What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves?
learning_targets: I can describe how energy moves through waves
I can compare transverse and longitudinal waves
sample_assessment:
In which type of wave do particles move perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer?
A) Longitudinal wave
B) Transverse wave
C) Sound wave
D) Compression wave

benchmark: Examine organisms or their structures using magnifying lenses and compound microscopes and describe cell structures and functions
item_specs: Students use microscopes to observe cells and identify structures and their functions
essential_questions: What are the basic structures of a cell?
How do cell structures relate to their functions?
learning_targets: I can use a microscope to observe cell structures
I can describe the function of major cell organelles
sample_assessment:
Which organelle controls the activities of the cell and contains DNA?
A) Cell membrane
B) Mitochondria
C) Nucleus
D) Ribosome

benchmark: Identify and describe the functions of major organ systems in the human body, including the circulatory, respiratory, and digestive systems
item_specs: Students identify organs within body systems and describe how systems work together
essential_questions: How do organ systems work together to maintain homeostasis?
What are the major functions of each body system?
learning_targets: I can identify the major organ systems and their functions
I can describe how body systems work together
sample_assessment:
Which organ system is primarily responsible for breaking down food into nutrients?
A) Circulatory system
B) Respiratory system
C) Digestive system
D) Nervous system

benchmark: Describe the structure and function of cell organelles, including the cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria, chloroplasts, and vacuoles
item_specs: Students match cell organelles to their structures and functions
essential_questions: What is the function of each cell organelle?
How do organelles work together to keep a cell alive?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure and function of major cell organelles
I can compare plant and animal cells
sample_assessment:
Which organelle is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
A) Nucleus
B) Mitochondria
C) Cell wall
D) Cell membrane

benchmark: Predict and describe how different types of organisms interact in an ecosystem, including competition, predation, parasitism, mutualism, and commensalism
item_specs: Students identify and describe types of ecological relationships between organisms
essential_questions: How do organisms interact in an ecosystem?
What are the different types of symbiotic relationships?
learning_targets: I can identify types of symbiotic relationships
I can predict how organisms interact within an ecosystem
sample_assessment:
A remora fish attaches to a shark and eats leftover food scraps. The shark is not harmed or helped. This is an example of:
A) Mutualism
B) Parasitism
C) Commensalism
D) Competition

benchmark: Recognize that inherited traits of individuals are governed by genetic material and that the instructions for traits are contained in genes within chromosomes
item_specs: Students describe the relationship between genes, chromosomes, and inherited traits
essential_questions: How are traits passed from parents to offspring?
What is the relationship between genes and chromosomes?
learning_targets: I can explain how genetic material determines traits
I can describe the relationship between genes, chromosomes, and DNA
sample_assessment:
Where are genes located?
A) In the cell membrane
B) In the cytoplasm
C) On chromosomes in the nucleus
D) In the mitochondria

benchmark: Describe the flow of energy through food webs, including the roles of the Sun, producers, consumers, and decomposers
item_specs: Students trace energy flow through ecosystems and identify trophic levels
essential_questions: How does energy flow through an ecosystem?
Why do food webs have fewer organisms at higher trophic levels?
learning_targets: I can trace energy flow through a food web
I can identify producers, consumers, and decomposers
sample_assessment:
In a food web, which organism is always at the base of the energy pyramid?
A) Decomposers
B) Herbivores
C) Producers
D) Carnivores

benchmark: Describe the structure of atoms, including the masses, electrical charges, and locations of protons, neutrons, and electrons
item_specs: Students identify the properties and locations of subatomic particles
essential_questions: What are atoms made of?
How are protons, neutrons, and electrons arranged in an atom?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure of an atom
I can identify the charge, mass, and location of subatomic particles
sample_assessment:
Which subatomic particle has a negative charge and is located outside the nucleus?
A) Proton
B) Neutron
C) Electron
D) Nucleus

benchmark: Interpret the arrangement of the Periodic Table, including groups and periods, to explain how properties are used to classify elements
item_specs: Students use the Periodic Table to identify properties and classify elements
essential_questions: How is the Periodic Table organized?
What can you learn about an element from its position on the Periodic Table?
learning_targets: I can use the Periodic Table to identify element properties
I can explain how elements are classified by groups and periods
sample_assessment:
Elements in the same group on the Periodic Table have similar:
A) Atomic masses
B) Numbers of neutrons
C) Chemical properties
D) Numbers of protons

benchmark: Interpret formulas of chemical compounds to determine the number and type of atoms in each compound
item_specs: Students read chemical formulas and identify the elements and quantities of atoms
essential_questions: What information does a chemical formula give you?
How do you read a chemical formula?
learning_targets: I can identify the elements in a chemical formula
I can determine the number of atoms of each element in a compound
sample_assessment:
How many total atoms are in one molecule of H2O?
A) 2
B) 3
C) 4
D) 1

benchmark: Describe the law of conservation of mass and demonstrate that mass is conserved in chemical reactions
item_specs: Students demonstrate understanding that matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions
essential_questions: What happens to mass during a chemical reaction?
How is the law of conservation of mass demonstrated?
learning_targets: I can explain the law of conservation of mass
I can demonstrate that mass is conserved in chemical reactions
sample_assessment:
If 10 grams of reactants undergo a chemical reaction, what is the total mass of the products?
A) Less than 10 grams
B) More than 10 grams
C) Exactly 10 grams
D) It depends on the reaction

benchmark: Describe the force of gravity and its effects on objects, including weight versus mass and gravitational interactions between objects
item_specs: Students distinguish between mass and weight and describe gravitational effects
essential_questions: What is the difference between mass and weight?
How does gravity affect objects?
learning_targets: I can explain the difference between mass and weight
I can describe how gravity affects objects
sample_assessment:
An astronaut has a mass of 70 kg on Earth. What is the astronaut's mass on the Moon?
A) 70 kg
B) 11.7 kg
C) 0 kg
D) 420 kg

benchmark: Demonstrate and calculate how unbalanced forces change the speed or direction of an object's motion using Newton's laws
item_specs: Students apply Newton's laws to explain and calculate changes in motion
essential_questions: How do forces affect the motion of objects?
What are Newton's three laws of motion?
learning_targets: I can explain Newton's three laws of motion
I can calculate the effect of unbalanced forces on motion
sample_assessment:
According to Newton's second law, if the force on an object doubles and the mass stays the same, the acceleration will:
A) Stay the same
B) Double
C) Be cut in half
D) Triple

benchmark: Model and describe the process of natural selection and how it leads to changes in populations over time
item_specs: Students explain natural selection and its role in the evolution of populations
essential_questions: How does natural selection lead to evolution?
What conditions are necessary for natural selection to occur?
learning_targets: I can describe the process of natural selection
I can explain how natural selection leads to changes in populations
sample_assessment:
In a population of beetles, green beetles blend in with leaves and are less likely to be eaten by birds. Over time, the population will most likely:
A) Have fewer green beetles
B) Have more green beetles
C) Stay exactly the same
D) All become brown

benchmark: Describe how plate tectonics causes major geological events such as ocean basin formation, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and mountain building
item_specs: Students connect plate tectonic processes to geological events and features
essential_questions: How do plate tectonics shape Earth's surface?
What causes earthquakes and volcanic eruptions?
learning_targets: I can explain how plate tectonics causes geological events
I can describe the three types of plate boundaries
sample_assessment:
Earthquakes most commonly occur at:
A) The center of tectonic plates
B) Plate boundaries
C) The equator
D) Random locations

benchmark: Describe the structure of DNA, including complementary base pairing, and explain its role in storing genetic information
item_specs: Students describe the double helix structure and base pairing rules of DNA
essential_questions: How does the structure of DNA allow it to store genetic information?
What are the base pairing rules?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure of DNA
I can apply complementary base pairing rules
sample_assessment:
In DNA, adenine (A) always pairs with:
A) Cytosine (C)
B) Guanine (G)
C) Thymine (T)
D) Uracil (U)

benchmark: Compare and contrast the processes of mitosis and meiosis, including their roles in growth, repair, and sexual reproduction
item_specs: Students compare mitosis and meiosis in terms of process, products, and function
essential_questions: How are mitosis and meiosis different?
Why are both types of cell division necessary?
learning_targets: I can compare the steps and outcomes of mitosis and meiosis
I can explain the role of each type of cell division
sample_assessment:
How many chromosomes do human gametes (sex cells) contain?
A) 46
B) 23
C) 92
D) 12

benchmark: Analyze the levels of organization in biological systems, including cells, tissues, organs, organ systems, and organisms
item_specs: Students identify and order the levels of biological organization
essential_questions: How are living things organized from simplest to most complex?
How do cells form tissues, organs, and systems?
learning_targets: I can describe the levels of organization from cells to organisms
I can explain how each level builds on the previous one
sample_assessment:
Which correctly shows the levels of organization from simplest to most complex?
A) Organ, tissue, cell, organ system, organism
B) Cell, tissue, organ, organ system, organism
C) Tissue, cell, organ system, organ, organism
D) Organism, organ system, organ, tissue, cell

benchmark: Describe the role of enzymes in cellular processes, including their function as biological catalysts
item_specs: Students describe how enzymes function and the factors that affect enzyme activity
essential_questions: What role do enzymes play in living organisms?
What factors affect enzyme function?
learning_targets: I can explain how enzymes speed up chemical reactions
I can describe factors that affect enzyme activity
sample_assessment:
Enzymes are important because they:
A) Provide energy for reactions
B) Speed up chemical reactions by lowering activation energy
C) Create new molecules from nothing
D) Store genetic information

benchmark: Compare and contrast photosynthesis and cellular respiration, including the reactants, products, and energy transformations
item_specs: Students compare the two major energy processes in living organisms
essential_questions: How are photosynthesis and cellular respiration related?
What are the inputs and outputs of each process?
learning_targets: I can compare photosynthesis and cellular respiration
I can describe the energy transformations in each process
sample_assessment:
Which statement correctly describes the relationship between photosynthesis and cellular respiration?
A) They occur in the same organelle
B) The products of one are the reactants of the other
C) They both require sunlight
D) They both produce oxygen

benchmark: Analyze the relationships between organisms in an ecosystem, including food webs, energy pyramids, and the cycling of matter
item_specs: Students analyze ecological relationships using food webs and energy pyramids
essential_questions: How does energy flow through ecosystems?
How is matter cycled in ecosystems?
learning_targets: I can analyze energy flow through food webs
I can describe how matter is cycled in ecosystems
sample_assessment:
In an energy pyramid, which trophic level has the most available energy?
A) Tertiary consumers
B) Secondary consumers
C) Primary consumers
D) Producers

benchmark: Describe the arrangement of the Periodic Table, including groups, periods, and electron configuration, to predict chemical and physical properties of elements
item_specs: Students use periodic trends to predict element properties
essential_questions: How does an element's position on the Periodic Table relate to its properties?
What periodic trends can be predicted?
learning_targets: I can use the Periodic Table to predict element properties
I can describe periodic trends including electronegativity and atomic radius
sample_assessment:
As you move from left to right across a period, atomic radius generally:
A) Increases
B) Decreases
C) Stays the same
D) Doubles

benchmark: Describe types of chemical bonds, including ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds, and their properties
item_specs: Students compare bond types and predict which type of bond will form between elements
essential_questions: What determines the type of bond that forms between atoms?
How do different bonds affect the properties of compounds?
learning_targets: I can describe ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds
I can predict the type of bond based on electronegativity
sample_assessment:
A bond between sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl) is classified as:
A) Covalent
B) Metallic
C) Ionic
D) Hydrogen

benchmark: Define and use the concept of the mole to perform stoichiometric calculations involving mass, moles, and number of particles
item_specs: Students convert between mass, moles, and number of particles using Avogadro's number
essential_questions: What is a mole and why is it useful in chemistry?
How do you convert between grams and moles?
learning_targets: I can convert between mass, moles, and number of particles
I can use molar mass to solve stoichiometry problems
sample_assessment:
How many moles are in 36 grams of water (H2O, molar mass = 18 g/mol)?
A) 1 mole
B) 2 moles
C) 18 moles
D) 36 moles

benchmark: Classify chemical reactions as synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, or combustion
item_specs: Students identify and classify chemical reactions by type
essential_questions: What are the five main types of chemical reactions?
How do you identify the type of a chemical reaction?
learning_targets: I can classify chemical reactions by type
I can predict the products of each reaction type
sample_assessment:
In the reaction 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O, what type of reaction is this?
A) Decomposition
B) Single replacement
C) Synthesis
D) Double replacement

benchmark: Describe the properties of solutions, including concentration, solubility, and the factors that affect the rate of dissolving
item_specs: Students describe solution properties and factors affecting dissolving rate
essential_questions: What factors affect how fast a solute dissolves?
How is the concentration of a solution measured?
learning_targets: I can describe factors that affect solubility and dissolving rate
I can calculate the concentration of a solution
sample_assessment:
Which action would increase the rate at which sugar dissolves in water?
A) Using cold water
B) Using less water
C) Stirring the water
D) Using larger sugar crystals

benchmark: Define acids and bases, describe their properties, and use the pH scale to classify solutions
item_specs: Students classify substances as acids or bases using pH values and properties
essential_questions: What are the characteristics of acids and bases?
How is the pH scale used to classify solutions?
learning_targets: I can classify solutions as acidic, basic, or neutral using the pH scale
I can describe the properties of acids and bases
sample_assessment:
A solution with a pH of 3 is classified as:
A) Strongly basic
B) Neutral
C) Weakly acidic
D) Strongly acidic

benchmark: Describe and calculate the relationships between displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time using kinematic equations
item_specs: Students use kinematic equations to solve motion problems
essential_questions: How do you describe the motion of an object mathematically?
What is the relationship between velocity, acceleration, and time?
learning_targets: I can calculate displacement, velocity, and acceleration
I can use kinematic equations to solve motion problems
sample_assessment:
A car accelerates from rest at 3 m/s^2 for 5 seconds. What is its final velocity?
A) 8 m/s
B) 15 m/s
C) 1.67 m/s
D) 25 m/s

benchmark: Apply Newton's laws of motion to analyze the forces acting on objects in various situations, including friction, tension, and gravitational force
item_specs: Students draw free body diagrams and apply Newton's laws to solve force problems
essential_questions: How do Newton's laws explain the motion of objects?
How do you analyze all forces acting on an object?
learning_targets: I can draw free body diagrams showing all forces on an object
I can apply F = ma to solve problems
sample_assessment:
A 10 kg box is pushed with a force of 50 N across a floor with 20 N of friction. What is the acceleration?
A) 5 m/s^2
B) 3 m/s^2
C) 7 m/s^2
D) 2 m/s^2

benchmark: Describe and calculate work, power, and the relationship between work and energy
item_specs: Students calculate work and power and explain the work-energy relationship
essential_questions: What is the scientific definition of work?
How are work, power, and energy related?
learning_targets: I can calculate work using W = Fd
I can calculate power and explain its relationship to work
sample_assessment:
How much work is done when a 100 N force pushes an object 5 meters?
A) 20 J
B) 105 J
C) 500 J
D) 95 J

benchmark: Describe the law of conservation of energy and analyze energy transformations in mechanical systems
item_specs: Students apply conservation of energy to analyze mechanical systems
essential_questions: What does the law of conservation of energy state?
How does energy transform in mechanical systems?
learning_targets: I can explain the law of conservation of energy
I can analyze energy transformations between kinetic and potential energy
sample_assessment:
A ball is dropped from a height. At what point is the kinetic energy greatest?
A) At the highest point
B) Halfway down
C) Just before hitting the ground
D) After it hits the ground

benchmark: Describe the characteristics and behavior of waves, including reflection, refraction, diffraction, and interference
item_specs: Students describe wave behaviors and their applications
essential_questions: How do waves behave when they encounter different media?
What are the differences between reflection, refraction, and diffraction?
learning_targets: I can describe wave behaviors including reflection, refraction, and diffraction
I can explain interference patterns
sample_assessment:
When light passes from air into water and bends, this is called:
A) Reflection
B) Refraction
C) Diffraction
D) Interference

benchmark: Describe the relationship between electric force, charge, and distance using Coulomb's law
item_specs: Students apply Coulomb's law to calculate electric forces between charges
essential_questions: How does electric force depend on charge and distance?
What is Coulomb's law?
learning_targets: I can describe the factors that affect electric force
I can apply Coulomb's law to calculate electric force
sample_assessment:
According to Coulomb's law, if the distance between two charges is doubled, the electric force:
A) Doubles
B) Quadruples
C) Is reduced to one-fourth
D) Is reduced to one-half

benchmark: Analyze the evidence for biological evolution, including fossil records, comparative anatomy, and molecular biology
item_specs: Students evaluate multiple lines of evidence supporting biological evolution
essential_questions: What evidence supports the theory of evolution?
How do fossils help us understand the history of life?
learning_targets: I can analyze evidence for evolution from multiple sources
I can explain how comparative anatomy supports evolution
sample_assessment:
The similar bone structure in a human arm, whale flipper, and bat wing is evidence of:
A) Convergent evolution
B) Homologous structures from a common ancestor
C) Analogous structures
D) No evolutionary relationship

benchmark: Describe the interactions between solar, weather, and ocean systems that determine climate and weather patterns
item_specs: Students explain how solar energy, weather, and ocean systems interact to create weather and climate
essential_questions: How does solar energy drive weather and climate?
How do ocean currents affect climate?
learning_targets: I can describe how solar energy drives weather patterns
I can explain how ocean currents influence climate
sample_assessment:
Coastal areas tend to have milder temperatures than inland areas because:
A) They get more sunlight
B) Ocean water absorbs and releases heat slowly, moderating temperature
C) They are at lower elevations
D) They have more vegetation

benchmark: Explore the characteristics of the solar system, including the Sun, planets, moons, asteroids, comets, and meteoroids
item_specs: Students describe characteristics of solar system objects and their relationships
essential_questions: What are the major objects in our solar system?
How do the planets compare to each other?
learning_targets: I can describe the characteristics of objects in the solar system
I can compare inner and outer planets
sample_assessment:
Which characteristic distinguishes the inner planets from the outer planets?
A) Inner planets are larger
B) Inner planets are made mostly of rock
C) Inner planets have more moons
D) Inner planets have rings

benchmark: Calculate average speed using distance and time measurements and represent motion on distance-time graphs
item_specs: Students calculate speed and interpret distance-time graphs
essential_questions: How do you calculate average speed?
What does a distance-time graph tell you about motion?
learning_targets: I can calculate average speed using the formula speed = distance / time
I can interpret distance-time graphs
sample_assessment:
A student walks 100 meters in 50 seconds. What is the average speed?
A) 5000 m/s
B) 2 m/s
C) 50 m/s
D) 0.5 m/s

benchmark: Describe the water cycle and explain how it relates to weather and climate
item_specs: Students describe the processes of the water cycle and their role in weather
essential_questions: How does the water cycle drive weather patterns?
What are the main processes of the water cycle?
learning_targets: I can describe the processes of the water cycle
I can explain how the water cycle relates to weather patterns
sample_assessment:
Which process in the water cycle is responsible for the formation of clouds?
A) Evaporation
B) Precipitation
C) Condensation
D) Runoff

benchmark: Recognize that the Sun provides the energy that drives convection within the atmosphere and oceans, producing winds and ocean currents
item_specs: Students explain how solar energy drives atmospheric and oceanic circulation
essential_questions: How does the Sun's energy create wind?
What causes ocean currents?
learning_targets: I can explain how solar energy drives convection
I can describe how convection creates winds and ocean currents
sample_assessment:
Winds are primarily caused by:
A) The Moon's gravitational pull
B) Unequal heating of Earth's surface by the Sun
C) Earth's magnetic field
D) Volcanic eruptions

benchmark: Describe the role of microorganisms in maintaining equilibrium during the carbon and nitrogen cycles
item_specs: Students explain the role of bacteria and other microorganisms in nutrient cycling
essential_questions: How do microorganisms contribute to nutrient cycling?
Why are bacteria important in the nitrogen cycle?
learning_targets: I can describe the role of decomposers in the carbon cycle
I can explain how nitrogen-fixing bacteria contribute to the nitrogen cycle
sample_assessment:
Nitrogen-fixing bacteria are important because they:
A) Release oxygen into the atmosphere
B) Convert atmospheric nitrogen into forms plants can use
C) Break down carbon dioxide
D) Produce glucose through photosynthesis

benchmark: Describe the gas laws (Boyle's, Charles's, and Gay-Lussac's) and apply them to solve problems involving pressure, temperature, and volume
item_specs: Students apply gas law relationships to solve problems
essential_questions: How are pressure, volume, and temperature of a gas related?
What happens to gas volume when pressure increases?
learning_targets: I can describe the relationship between pressure and volume (Boyle's Law)
I can apply gas laws to solve problems
sample_assessment:
According to Boyle's Law, if the pressure on a gas doubles (at constant temperature), the volume will:
A) Double
B) Stay the same
C) Be cut in half
D) Triple

benchmark: Calculate the rate of a chemical reaction and describe factors that affect reaction rates, including temperature, concentration, surface area, and catalysts
item_specs: Students identify and explain factors affecting chemical reaction rates
essential_questions: What factors affect the speed of a chemical reaction?
How do catalysts speed up reactions?
learning_targets: I can describe factors that affect reaction rates
I can explain how catalysts work
sample_assessment:
Which change would increase the rate of a chemical reaction?
A) Lowering the temperature
B) Decreasing the concentration of reactants
C) Adding a catalyst
D) Using larger pieces of solid reactant

benchmark: Describe the basic properties of electric circuits, including series and parallel circuits, and calculate voltage, current, and resistance using Ohm's law
item_specs: Students compare circuit types and apply Ohm's law to solve problems
essential_questions: What is the difference between series and parallel circuits?
How are voltage, current, and resistance related?
learning_targets: I can compare series and parallel circuits
I can use Ohm's law (V = IR) to solve circuit problems
sample_assessment:
Using Ohm's law, if a circuit has a voltage of 12 V and a resistance of 4 ohms, what is the current?
A) 48 A
B) 3 A
C) 16 A
D) 8 A

benchmark: Describe the structure and function of the four major macromolecules (carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids) in living organisms
item_specs: Students identify the structure, function, and examples of each macromolecule
essential_questions: What are the four major macromolecules?
What role does each macromolecule play in living organisms?
learning_targets: I can identify the four macromolecules and their functions
I can describe the monomers that make up each macromolecule
sample_assessment:
Which macromolecule is made up of amino acids and functions in structure, transport, and catalysis?
A) Carbohydrates
B) Lipids
C) Proteins
D) Nucleic acids

benchmark: Describe the properties of magnets and the relationship between electricity and magnetism, including electromagnets and electromagnetic induction
item_specs: Students describe magnetic properties and the connection between electricity and magnetism
essential_questions: How are electricity and magnetism related?
What is electromagnetic induction?
learning_targets: I can describe the relationship between electricity and magnetism
I can explain how electromagnets and generators work
sample_assessment:
An electromagnet differs from a permanent magnet because:
A) It is always stronger
B) Its magnetism can be turned on and off with electric current
C) It does not have a magnetic field
D) It only attracts aluminum

benchmark: Predict the results of modifying factors within an ecosystem, including the impact of human activities on food webs and natural cycles
item_specs: Students predict consequences of changes to ecosystem components
essential_questions: How do changes in one part of an ecosystem affect the whole system?
What impact do human activities have on ecosystems?
learning_targets: I can predict how changes to one organism affect the food web
I can describe the impact of human activities on ecosystems
sample_assessment:
If a disease kills most of the rabbits in an ecosystem, what would most likely happen to the hawk population that feeds on them?
A) The hawk population would increase
B) The hawk population would decrease
C) The hawk population would not change
D) The hawks would become herbivores
